text.title: Microservices Antipatterns
text.summary: Two critical microservices violations: distributed monolith via synchronous chains, and shared database between services.

antipattern.description: Services call each other synchronously for every operation, creating a chain where all services must be up for any request to succeed.
antipattern.reason: Availability multiplies: if each service is 99% up, a chain of 5 synchronous calls is only 95% available. This defeats the resilience benefit of microservices.

example.code: class OrderService: async def create_order(self, data): # Use locally cached/denormalized data — no sync calls user_valid = await self.user_cache.exists(data.user_id) product = await self.product_cache.get(data.product_id) order = Order.create(data) await self.repository.save(order) await self.event_bus.publish(OrderCreated(order)) # Other services react async

antipattern.description: Multiple services query the same database tables directly, sharing schema ownership implicitly.
antipattern.reason: Schema changes in one service break all other services sharing the table. This creates a hidden coupling that makes independent deployment impossible.

example.code: # user-service and order-service both SELECT from the same 'users' table # A column rename in user-service breaks order-service at runtime

# user-service owns 'users' table # order-service has 'customer_info' with denormalized user data # populated via events: UserCreated, UserEmailChanged # Schema changes in user-service only require order-service to subscribe to new events
